name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
أَفَمَن كَانَ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ وَيَتْلُوهُ شَاهِدٌ مِّنْهُ وَمِن قَبْلِهِۦ كِتَـٰبُ مُوسَىٰٓ إِمَامًا وَرَحْمَةً ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ ۚ وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ فَٱلنَّارُ مَوْعِدُهُۥ ۚ فَلَا تَكُ فِى مِرْيَةٍ مِّنْهُ ۚ إِنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٧)
```

```english
Is he who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord, and whom a witness of his own [family] follows? And before him there was the Book of Moses, a guide and mercy. It is they who have faith in it, and whoever defies him from among the factions, the Fire is their tryst. So do not be in doubt about it; it is the truth from your Lord, but most people do not have faith. (17)
```

```arabic
وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ وَيَقُولُ ٱلْأَشْهَـٰدُ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا۟ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ ۚ أَلَا لَعْنَةُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٨)
```

```english
Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah? Such shall be presented before their Lord, and the witnesses will say, ‘It is these who lied against their Lord.’ Behold! The curse of Allah is upon the wrongdoers (18)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (١٩)
```

```english
—those who bar [others] from the way of Allah, and seek to make it crooked, and disbelieve in the Hereafter. (19)
```

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَمْ يَكُونُوا۟ مُعْجِزِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا كَانَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِنْ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۘ يُضَـٰعَفُ لَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ ۚ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَسْتَطِيعُونَ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يُبْصِرُونَ (٢٠)
```

```english
They cannot frustrate [Allah] on the earth, nor do they have besides Allah any guardian. For them the punishment shall be doubled, for they could neither listen, nor did they use to see. (20)
```

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٢١)
```

```english
They are the ones who have ruined their souls, and what they used to fabricate has forsaken them. (21)
```

```arabic
لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمُ ٱلْأَخْسَرُونَ (٢٢)
```

```english
Undoubtedly, they are the ones who will be the biggest losers in the Hereafter. (22)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَأَخْبَتُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢٣)
```

```english
Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds and are humble before their Lord—they shall be the inhabitants of paradise, and they shall remain in it [forever]. (23)
```

```arabic
۞ مَثَلُ ٱلْفَرِيقَيْنِ كَٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْأَصَمِّ وَٱلْبَصِيرِ وَٱلسَّمِيعِ ۚ هَلْ يَسْتَوِيَانِ مَثَلًا ۚ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٢٤)
```

```english
The parable of the two parties is that of one who is blind and deaf and one who sees and hears. Are they equal in comparison? Will you not then take admonition? (24)
```

## (Commentary)

The apparent context of the verses is that they serve to comfort the soul of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), strengthen his faith in the Book of Allah, and affirm the insight (البصيرة) he possesses regarding his mission. The discourse continues in the same mode of addressing him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); the speech was directed toward him until it reached the point of what they accused him of—fabricating lies against Allah, the Glorified. Thus, He commanded him to challenge them to bring ten fabricated surahs like it. Then, He commanded him to be of good cheer and to remain firm in the knowledge he possesses—that it is revealed from Allah—for he is indeed upon the truth and is not a fabricator. Therefore, he should not feel desolated by the turning away of the majority, nor should he doubt.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَيَتْلُوهُ شاهِدٌ مِنْهُ وَمِنْ قَبْلِهِ كِتابُ مُوسى إِماماً وَرَحْمَةً** — *Is he who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord, and whom a witness from him follows, and before him was the Book of Moses, a guide and a mercy?* (11:17). This sentence is a derivation from the preceding discourse, which functions as an argument for the Quran being a Book revealed from Allah, the Glorified. The word "who" (مَنْ) is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is omitted; the estimated meaning is: "Is he like others?" or something to that effect. The evidence for this is His subsequent saying: **أُولئِكَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ وَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِهِ مِنَ الْأَحْزابِ فَالنَّارُ مَوْعِدُهُ** — *They believe in it, and whoever of the factions disbelieves in it, the Fire is his promised place.*

The interrogative is one of denial (إنكاري), and the meaning is: One who is such-and-such is not like others who are not so; and since you possess these attributes, do not be in doubt concerning the Quran.

Regarding His saying: **عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ** — *on a manifest proof from his Lord*, the manifest proof (البينة) — *the clear evidence that makes the truth distinct* — is an adjective meaning that which is apparent and clear. However, apparent and clear things often clarify what is joined to them or related to them, much like light, which is manifest (بين) in itself and makes other things manifest. For this reason, the term "manifest proof" (البينة) is frequently used for that by which something else is made clear, such as an argument (الحجة) or a sign (الآية). A witness to a claimant's case is also called a "manifest proof" (بينة).

Allah, the Exalted, has called an argument a "manifest proof" (بينة), as in His saying: **لِيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ** — *so that he who perishes might perish by a manifest proof* (8:42). He also called His sign a "manifest proof," as in His saying: **قَدْ جاءَتْكُمْ بَيِّنَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ هذِهِ ناقَةُ اللهِ لَكُمْ آيَةً** — *There has certainly come to you a manifest proof from your Lord. This is the she-camel of Allah, a sign for you* (7:73). He also called the specific divine insight (البصيرة) granted to the prophets a "manifest proof," as in His saying, narrating from Noah (عليه السلام): **يا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي وَآتانِي رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِهِ** — *O my people! Tell me, should I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, and He has granted me a mercy from Himself* (11:28). Or it refers to absolute divine insight, as is apparent in His saying, the Exalted:

**أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ كَمَنْ زُيِّنَ لَهُ سُوءُ عَمَلِهِ وَاتَّبَعُوا أَهْواءَهُمْ** — *Is he who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord like those to whom the evil of their conduct has been made to seem fair and who follow their desires?* (47:14). Regarding its meaning, He has also said: **أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُماتِ لَيْسَ بِخارِجٍ مِنْها** — *Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people, like one who is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?* (6:122).

It appears that the intended meaning of "manifest proof" (البينة) here is this latter general sense, based on the context of His subsequent saying: **أُولئِكَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ** — *They believe in it*, even if the specific instance intended is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), for the discourse is structured so that His saying: **فَلا تَكُ فِي مِرْيَةٍ مِنْهُ** — *so do not be in doubt about it* — may be derived from it.

Thus, what is intended is the divine insight (البصيرة) granted to the Prophet (عليه السلام), not the Quran itself revealed to him. This is because it would not be outwardly appropriate to derive the command "do not be in doubt about it" from the Quran itself when the Quran is the very thing being doubted. This is clear, and it does not contradict the fact that the Quran is in itself a manifest proof from Allah by virtue of being a sign (آية) from Him, as in His saying: **قُلْ إِنِّي عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِِّي وَكَذَّبْتُمْ بِهِ** — *Say, ‘Indeed I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord, and you have denied it’* (6:57), for that context is different from this one.

From what has passed, it becomes clear that the view of those who say that "he who stands..." refers exclusively to the Prophet as the primary linguistic intent (إرادة استعمالية) is misplaced; rather, he is intended in terms of the application to the specific case (انطباق المورد). Similarly, the view of those who say it refers to the believers among the companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is also without evidence for such restriction.

It also reveals the invalidity of the view that "manifest proof" (البينة) refers to the Quran, as well as the view that it refers to the proof of reason (حجة العقل) and is attributed to the Lord because He establishes both rational and traditional evidences. The reason for its invalidity is that there is no evidence for such restriction, and the manifest proof established for the Prophet (عليه السلام) from Allah's side through divine introduction (التعريف الإلهي) cannot be compared to the proof established for us through our intellects.

Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَتْلُوهُ شاهِدٌ مِنْهُ** — *and whom a witness from him follows*, the intended meaning of witnessing (الشهادة) is the bearing of testimony (تأدية الشهادة) which confirms the validity of the matter witnessed, rather than the mere receiving of it (تحملها). This is because the context is one of affirming the truth of the Quran, which is suitable for witnessing in the sense of bearing testimony, not merely receiving it.

It appears that this witness (شاهد) refers to someone who attained certainty in the truth of the Quran and possessed divine insight regarding it, thus believing in it out of insight and testifying that it is the truth revealed from Allah, just as one testifies to monotheism (التوحيد) and the messengership (الرسالة). Indeed, the testimony of a person of certainty and insight regarding a matter removes from a person the desolation of doubt and the uncertainty of being alone. For when a person adheres to a matter and is solitary in it, that solitude may cause him distress if no one supports him in his claim. However, if others among the people affirm it and support his view, his distress vanishes, his heart is strengthened, and his resolve is fortified. Allah has argued using a similar meaning in His saying: **قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كَانَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ وَكَفَرْتُمْ بِهِ وَشَهِدَ شَاهِدٌ مِنْ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ عَلَى مِثْلِهِ فَآمَنَ وَاسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ** — *Say, ‘Tell me, if it is from Allah and you disbelieve in it, and a witness from the Children of Israel has testified to its like and believed, while you are disdainful?’* (46:10).

Accordingly, His saying: **يَتْلُوهُ** — *follows him* — is derived from "following" (التلو), not from "recitation" (التلاوة). The pronoun in it refers back to "he who" (فَمَنْ) or to "manifest proof" (بَيِّنَةٍ) considering it as a light or an evidence. The outcome of both views is the same, for the witness who follows the possessor of the manifest proof follows his proof just as he follows the person himself. The pronoun in His saying: **مِنْهُ** — *from him* — refers back to "he who" (فَمَنْ) rather than "his Lord" (رَبِّهِ), and its not referring to "manifest proof" is obvious. The summary of the meaning is: One who possesses divine insight regarding a matter, and is joined by one who is of his own kind (مِنْ نَفْسِهِ) who testifies to the truth and uprightness of his affair.

Based on this interpretation, what has been narrated in the reports of both sects—that the witness refers to Ali (عليه السلام)—is applicable, provided it is understood as the intended meaning in terms of the application to the specific case (انطباق المورد) rather than the primary linguistic intent.

The commentators have various opinions regarding the meaning of this sentence. It has been said that "follows" (يتلو) is from "recitation" (التلاوة), just as it has been said it is from "following" (التلو). It has been said that the pronoun in "follows him" (يَتْلُوهُ) refers to "manifest proof" (بَيِّنَةٍ), just as it has been said it refers to "he who" (فَمَنْ).

It has been said the witness is the Quran; it has been said it is Gabriel reciting the Quran to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), perhaps derived from His saying: **لكِنِ اللهُ يَشْهَدُ بِما أَنْزَلَ إِلَيْكَ أَنْزَلَهُ بِعِلْمِهِ وَالْمَلائِكَةُ يَشْهَدُونَ** — *But Allah bears witness to what He has sent down to you—He sent it down with His knowledge—and the angels bear witness* (4:166). It has been said the witness is an angel who supports the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and preserves the Quran for him, perhaps based on some reliance on the aforementioned verse.

It has been said the witness is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) himself, as Allah has said: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ إِنَّا أَرْسَلْناكَ شاهِداً وَمُبَشِّراً وَنَذِيراً** — *O Prophet! Indeed We have sent you as a witness, as a bearer of good news and as a warner* (33:45). It has been said "a witness from him" means his tongue, i.e., he recites the Quran with his tongue.

It has been said the witness is Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام), and several narrations have been reported to this effect through both Shi'a and Sunni chains.

Reflection on the context of the verse and the apparent meaning of its sentences is sufficient to invalidate these views other than the meaning we have provided; thus, we shall not prolong the discussion by examining or critiquing them.

Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَمِنْ قَبْلِهِ كِتابُ مُوسى إِماماً وَرَحْمَةً** — *and before him was the Book of Moses, a guide and a mercy*, the pronoun refers to the relative pronoun "who" (الموصول) or to the "manifest proof" (البينة) in the same manner we mentioned regarding the pronoun in "follows him" (يَتْلُوهُ). The sentence is a circumstantial clause (حال) following another; that is: Is he who possesses divine insight through which it is disclosed to him that the Quran is the truth revealed from Allah—while there is with him a witness from him who testifies to that out of insight, and while this person who stands on a manifest proof was preceded by the Book of Moses as a guide and a mercy—or before his manifest proof, which includes the Quran or is the Quran containing the knowledge and laws that guide to the truth, was the Book of Moses as a guide? Thus, he or the manifest proof he possesses is not an unprecedented matter without a prior example or peer; rather, there is a path already trodden that the Book of Moses guides toward.

From here, the reason for describing the Book of Moses—the Torah—as a guide (إمام) and a mercy (رحمة) becomes clear: it contains true knowledge and divine law that is followed (يؤتم به) and whose blessings are enjoyed. Allah has mentioned it with this description in another place in His speech, saying: **قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كَانَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ وَكَفَرْتُمْ بِهِ وَشَهِدَ شَاهِدٌ مِنْ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ عَلَى مِثْلِهِ فَآمَنَ وَاسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ** — until He said — **وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَوْ كَانَ خَيْرًا مَا سَبَقُونَا إِلَيْهِ وَإِذْ لَمْ يَهْتَدُوا بِهِ فَسَيَقُولُونَ هَذَا إِفْكٌ قَدِيمٌ * وَمِنْ قَبْلِهِ كِتَابُ مُوسَى إِمَامًا وَرَحْمَةً وَهَذَا كِتَابٌ مُصَدِّقٌ لِسَانًا عَرَبِيًّا لِيُنْذِرَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا وَبُشْرَى لِلْمُحْسِنِينَ** — *Say, ‘Tell me, if it is from Allah and you disbelieve in it, and a witness from the Children of Israel has testified to its like and believed, while you are disdainful?’ ... The faithless say of the faithful, ‘Had it been [any] good, they would not have taken the lead over us in [accepting] it.’ And since they could not be guided by it, they will say, ‘This is an ancient fiction.’ Before it the Book of Moses was a guide and a mercy, and this is a Book confirming [it], in the Arabic language, to warn those who do wrong, and as a good news for the virtuous* (46:10-12).

These verses—as you can see—are the closest in content to the verse under discussion. They mention first that the Quran is a divine manifest proof or a matter established by divine manifest proof; then they mention the testimony of a witness from the Children of Israel and his support for it; then they mention that it was preceded in the knowledge and laws it contains by the Book of Moses, which was a guide and a mercy followed and used for guidance by the people, a trodden and tested path; and the Quran is a Book like it, confirming it, revealed from Allah to warn the wrongdoers and give good news to the virtuous.

From here it also becomes clear that His saying: **إِماماً وَرَحْمَةً** — *a guide and a mercy* — is a circumstantial clause (حال) modifying "the Book of Moses," not "a witness from him," as some have mentioned.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أُولئِكَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ وَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِهِ مِنَ الْأَحْزابِ فَالنَّارُ مَوْعِدُهُ** — *They believe in it, and whoever of the factions disbelieves in it, the Fire is his promised place.* Those referred to by "They" (أُولئِكَ), based on the preceding meaning of the verse's opening, are those who stand on a manifest proof from their Lord, indicated by His saying: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ** — *Is he who...* As for referring the demonstrative to the believers because the context points to them, it is far-fetched.

Similarly, the pronoun in His saying: **بِهِ** — *in it* — refers to the Quran because it is a manifest proof from Him, the Exalted, or a matter established by manifest proof. As for referring it to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), it does not harmonize with the meaning of the verse we have established. For the beginning of the verse explains the state of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in a general manner so that His saying: **فَلا تَكُ فِي مِرْيَةٍ مِنْهُ** — *so do not be in doubt about it* — may be derived from it. It is as if it were said: You are upon such a manifest proof, and with you is a witness, and before you was the Book of Moses; and whoever possesses these attributes believes in the Book of Allah granted to him. It would not be correct to say: "Whoever possesses these attributes believes in you." The discussion regarding the pronoun in **وَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِهِ** — *and whoever disbelieves in it* — is the same as the discussion regarding the pronoun in **يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ** — *they believe in it*.

The matter of this verse regarding the possibilities of its individual words and pronouns is remarkable; multiplying them by one another reaches thousands of possibilities, some of which are correct and others not.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلا تَكُ فِي مِرْيَةٍ مِنْهُ إِنَّهُ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** — *So do not be in doubt about it; it is the truth from your Lord, but most of the people do not have faith* (11:17). Doubt (المرية) is a type of uncertainty (الشك). The sentence is a derivation from the opening of the verse. The meaning is that one who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord in a matter, and a witness from him has testified to it, and before it was a guide and a mercy like the Book of Moses, is not like other heedless and deceived people; he believes in what he possesses of Allah's command and is not desolated by the turning away of most people from what he has. You are likewise, for you stand on a manifest proof from your Lord, a witness follows you, and before you was the Book of Moses as a guide and a mercy. Since this is the case, do not be in doubt regarding what has been revealed to you of the Quran; it is pure truth from Allah's side, but most people do not believe.

His saying: **إِنَّهُ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ** — *it is the truth from your Lord* — is a justification for the prohibition, emphasized with "indeed" (إن) and the definite article (لام الجنس) to indicate the abundance of reasons that negate doubt, namely the establishment of manifest proof, the testimony of the witness, and the precedence of the Book of Moses as a guide and a mercy.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً** — *Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah?* (11:18) until the end of the verse. It is possible that this is a continuation of the previous context, serving to comfort the soul of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The meaning would then be: Since you stand on a manifest proof from your Lord, you are not a wrongdoer; far be it from you to be a fabricator of lies against Allah, because the one who fabricates a lie against Allah is the most unjust of wrongdoers, and they shall have such-and-such of the consequences of their lies.

In any case, the intended meaning of fabricating a lie against Allah, the Glorified, is to describe Him with what is not in Him, or to attribute something to Him unjustly or without knowledge. Fabrication (الافتراء) is among the most manifest forms of wrongdoing (الظلم) and sin (الإثم). Wrongdoing increases in magnitude according to the magnitude of its object, such that when it reaches the threshold of Grandeur and Majesty, it is among the greatest of wrongs.

The discourse functions as a reversal of the claim against them. Since they used to say to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he fabricated a lie against Allah by attributing the Quran to Him, the statement is turned back upon them: they are the ones who fabricated lies against Allah when they affirmed partners for Him without knowledge—and He is Allah, there is no god but He; and when they barred from the way of Allah—which means denying it is the way of Allah, and that is a fabrication; and when they sought another way and adopted it as a custom in their lives—which was an alteration of the way of Allah to which the primordial nature (الفطرة) and prophethood (النبوة) guide; and when they disbelieved in the Hereafter and denied it—which is an affirmation of a beginning without a return and an attribution of futility and vain action to Him, the Exalted, which is a fabrication against Him.

In summary, their adoption of a religion other than Allah's religion and creed, their embracing of false beliefs regarding the Beginning and the Return, and their following a custom other than Allah's custom in their worldly social lives—whereas that which is from Allah is only the truth, and there is no custom with Allah except the religion of truth—is a fabrication against Allah. The witnesses (الأشهاد) shall testify against them for that on the day they are presented before their Lord.

His saying, the Exalted: **أُولئِكَ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلى رَبِّهِمْ** — *They shall be presented before their Lord.* Presentation (العرض) is the showing of a thing so that it may be seen and known. Since the lifting of the veils between them and their Lord on the Day of Resurrection—through the appearance of His signs and the clarity of the manifest truth without any distraction—is a compulsory presence for the passing of judgment, He called it a "presentation" of them before their Lord. Just as He called it in another way a "coming forth" (بروز) of them to Allah, saying: **يَوْمَ هُمْ بارِزُونَ لا يَخْفى عَلَى اللهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ** — *The day when they will emerge [from their graves], nothing about them will be hidden from Allah* (40:16), and He said: **وَبَرَزُوا لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ** — *and they will emerge before Allah, the One, the All-paramount* (14:48). Thus He said: **أُولئِكَ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلى رَبِّهِمْ** — meaning the angels entrusted with them shall bring them and make them stand in a station where there is no veil or barrier between them and their Lord for the passing of judgment.

His saying: **وَيَقُولُ الْأَشْهادُ هؤُلاءِ الَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا عَلى رَبِّهِمْ** — *and the witnesses will say, ‘These are they who lied against their Lord.’* The witnesses (الأشهاد) is the plural of "witness" (شهيد), like "nobles" (أشراف) is the plural of "noble" (شريف). It has been said it is the plural of "witness" (شاهد), like "companions" (أصحاب) is the plural of "companion" (صاحب). The first view is supported by His saying, the Exalted: **فَكَيْفَ إِذا جِئْنا مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ بِشَهِيدٍ** — *So how will it be when We bring a witness from every nation* (4:41), and His saying: **وَجاءَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَعَها سائِقٌ وَشَهِيدٌ** — *Every soul will come accompanied by a driver and a witness* (50:21).

The saying of the witnesses, "These are they who lied against their Lord," is a testimony from them against the fabricators. That is, it is recorded against them that they are the fabricators by virtue of the testimony of the witnesses in a station where nothing but the truth is mentioned, and where there is no escape from confession and acceptance, as He, the Exalted, said: **لا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ وَقالَ صَواباً** — *they will not speak, except him whom the All-beneficent permits and who says what is right* (78:38), and He, the Exalted, said: **يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُحْضَراً وَما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ سُوءٍ تَوَدُّ لَوْ أَنَّ بَيْنَها وَبَيْنَهُ أَمَداً بَعِيداً** — *The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil it has done, it will wish there were a great distance between it and that [day]* (3:30).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَلا لَعْنَةُ اللهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ** — *Behold! The curse of Allah is upon the wrongdoers, who bar [others] from the way of Allah* (11:18-19) etc., is the completion of the speech of the witnesses. The evidence for this is His saying, the Exalted: **فَأَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ بَيْنَهُمْ أَنْ لَعْنَةُ اللهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَيَبْغُونَها عِوَجاً وَهُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ كافِرُونَ** — *Thereupon a caller will announce in their midst, ‘The curse of Allah be on the wrongdoers, who bar [others] from the way of Allah and seek to make it crooked, and who disbelieve in the Hereafter’* (7:44-45).

This statement of theirs, narrated in His speech, is an affirmation of distance and a curse upon the wrongdoers and a recording of the punishment. Cursing (اللعن) and mercy (الرحمة) on the Day of Resurrection are not like cursing and mercy in this world, as in His saying: **أُولئِكَ يَلْعَنُهُمُ اللهُ وَيَلْعَنُهُمُ اللَّاعِنُونَ** — *they are the ones whom Allah curses, and whom the cursers curse* (2:159). This is because the world is the abode of action and the Day of Resurrection is the day of recompense; thus, whatever curse or mercy is in it is the delivery of what was stored for them. The curse of a curser upon someone on the Day of Resurrection is his expulsion from the mercy of Allah specific to the believers and the recording of the punishment of distance upon him.

Then He, the Glorified, explained the "wrongdoers" by His saying, narrating from them: **الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَيَبْغُونَها عِوَجاً وَهُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ كافِرُونَ** — *who bar [others] from the way of Allah and seek to make it crooked, and who disbelieve in the Hereafter.* They are those who do not acknowledge the Day of Reckoning so as to work for it; rather, they work only for the world and follow a path of life through which they enjoy only the material world. This is the social custom (السنة الاجتماعية) that pays no heed to what Allah desires from His servants of the religion of truth and the creed of the primordial nature. These people—whether they believe in a Creator and follow a custom distorted and deviated from the religion of the primordial nature, which is Islam, or they do not believe in Him, like those who say: "There is nothing but our worldly life; we die and we live, and nothing destroys us but time"—are wrongdoers who fabricate lies against Allah. Some discussion related to these meanings has already passed in Surah al-A'raf, verses 44-45.

It has become clear from the preceding discussion in the two verses: first, that religion (الدين) in the terminology of the Quran is the social custom (السنة الاجتماعية) prevalent in society.

Second, that social customs are either a true, primordial religion, which is Islam, or a religion distorted from the true religion and the way of Allah, made crooked.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أُولئِكَ لَمْ يَكُونُوا مُعْجِزِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَما كانَ لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مِنْ أَوْلِياءَ** — *They cannot frustrate [Allah] on the earth, nor do they have any guardians besides Allah* (11:20) until the end of the verse. The reference is to the fabricators against Allah described in the two preceding verses.

The context indicates that the meaning of their not being able to frustrate [Allah] on the earth is that they were not frustrating Allah, the Glorified, in their earthly lives when they exited the garment of servitude and began fabricating lies against Allah, barring from His way, and seeking to make it crooked. All of that was not because their borrowed power surpassed the power of Allah, the Glorified, or their will preceded His will, nor because they exited the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah and entered the guardianship of another—those whom they took as guardians from their idols and other causes they relied upon. That is His saying: **وَما كانَ لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مِنْ أَوْلِياءَ** — *nor do they have any guardians besides Allah*.

In summary, neither did their power overcome the power of Allah, the Glorified, nor were their partners—whom they called guardians for themselves—truly guardians for them, managing their affairs and compelling them toward the rebellion and wrongdoing they committed. Rather, Allah, the Glorified, is their Guardian and the Manager of their affairs; He recompenses them for their evil intentions and deeds with that which drags them toward an evil punishment and leads them on step by step (يستدرجهم) from where they do not perceive, as He, the Exalted, said: **فَلَمَّا زاغُوا أَزاغَ اللهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ** — *So when they deviated, Allah made their hearts deviate* (61:5), and He said: **يُضِلُّ بِهِ كَثِيراً وَيَهْدِي بِهِ كَثِيراً وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ** — *He misleads many by it and guides many by it, and He misleads no one by it except the transgressors* (2:26).

His saying: **يُضاعَفُ لَهُمُ الْعَذابُ** — *The punishment will be doubled for them.* This is because they transgressed and then persisted in it, or because they disobeyed Allah themselves and led others to disobey Allah, so the punishment is doubled for them just as they doubled the disobedience. He, the Exalted, said: **لِيَحْمِلُوا أَوْزارَهُمْ كامِلَةً يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَمِنْ أَوْزارِ الَّذِينَ يُضِلُّونَهُمْ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ** — *that they may bear their burdens in full on the Day of Resurrection, along with some of the burdens of those whom they mislead without any knowledge* (16:25), and He said: **وَنَكْتُبُ ما قَدَّمُوا وَآثارَهُمْ** — *and We write what they have sent ahead and their footsteps* (36:12).

His saying: **ما كانُوا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ السَّمْعَ وَما كانُوا يُبْصِرُونَ** — *They could not hear, nor did they use to see.* This is in the position of justification (التعليل), which is why it is presented as a separate sentence (الفصل). He, the Exalted, says that they did not disbelieve or disobey because their will prevailed over Allah's will, nor because they had guardians besides Allah to support them against Him; rather, it was because they were unable to hear the warning and good news that came to them from His side, or the encouragement and deterrence mentioned to them from Him, and they did not see His signs so as to believe in them. This is as He described them in His saying: **لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ لا يَفْقَهُونَ بِها وَلَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ لا يُبْصِرُونَ بِها وَلَهُمْ آذانٌ لا يَسْمَعُونَ بِها أُولئِكَ كَالْأَنْعامِ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ** — *They have hearts with which they do not understand, and they have eyes with which they do not see, and they have ears with which they do not hear. They are like cattle; rather, they are more astray* (7:179). And in His saying: **وَنُقَلِّبُ أَفْئِدَتَهُمْ وَأَبْصارَهُمْ كَما لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا بِهِ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ** — *We shall turn their hearts and their sights, just as they did not believe in it the first time* (6:110). And His saying: **خَتَمَ اللهُ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ وَعَلى سَمْعِهِمْ وَعَلى أَبْصارِهِمْ غِشاوَةٌ** — *Allah has set a seal on their hearts and their hearing, and there is a blindfold on their sight* (2:7). There are many other verses indicating that He, the Exalted, stripped them of their intellects, eyes, and ears. However, He, the Exalted, narrates from them statements such as: **وَقالُوا لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ ما كُنَّا فِي أَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ فَاعْتَرَفُوا بِذَنْبِهِمْ** — *And they will say, ‘Had we listened or exercised our reason, we would not have been among the inmates of the Blaze.’ Thus they will confess their sin* (67:10-11). Their confession that their lack of hearing and reasoning was a sin—despite it being attributed to His stripping those faculties from them—indicates that they themselves sought the removal of these blessings through their sins, as indicated by the aforementioned saying of the Exalted: **وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ** — *and He misleads no one by it except the transgressors* (2:26), and others.

Regarding the meaning of His saying: **ما كانُوا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ السَّمْعَ وَما كانُوا يُبْصِرُونَ**, other views have been mentioned:

Among them: that His saying: "They could not..." etc., is in the accusative case (النصب) by the removal of the preposition (بنزع الخافض) and is connected to His saying: "The punishment will be doubled..." etc. The original would be: "because of what they were able to hear... and because of what they were able to see." The meaning is: the punishment is doubled for them because they were able to hear but did not hear, and because they were able to see but did not see.

Among them: that He intended by His saying: "They could not..." etc., to negate hearing and sight from their gods and idols. The estimation of the speech would be: those disbelievers and their gods were not frustrating [Allah] on the earth; and He said, informing about the gods: "They could not hear, nor did they use to see."

Among them: that the word "not" (ما) in "They could not" is not for negation but functions like the saying: "I shall surely stay with you as long as (ما) the star shines." The meaning is that they are punished as long as they are alive.

Among them: that the negation of hearing and sight means the negation of benefit. Because they found it burdensome to listen to the signs of Allah and look into them, and they disliked doing so, they were treated like those who cannot hear or see. Thus, the speech is metaphorical (كناية).

The most balanced of these views is the last one, and all of them are clearly weak; the correct view is what we have previously provided.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ** — *They are the ones who have ruined their souls, and what they used to fabricate has forsaken them* (11:21). As for their ruin (خسران), a human being truly possesses nothing—and that is by Allah's granting of ownership—except his own soul. When he buys for his soul that which contains its destruction and loss through disbelief and disobedience, he has ruined his soul in this transaction he undertook. Thus, the ruin of the soul is a metaphor for destruction. As for the forsaking (ضلال) of what they used to fabricate, it was a lie and fabrication that had no existence in reality outside of their illusions and claims, which were beautified for them by worldly desires and whims. With the folding up of the carpet of worldly life, those illusions vanish and disappear, and the lies and fabrications that appeared and settled within them go astray. On that day, they shall know that Allah is the Manifest Truth, and there shall appear to them from Allah what they had not expected.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لا جَرَمَ أَنَّهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ هُمُ الْأَخْسَرُونَ** — *Undoubtedly, they are the ones who will be the greatest losers in the Hereafter* (11:22). From al-Farra: "Undoubtedly" (لا جرم) originally meant "no escape" and "inevitably," then it became frequent and was shifted to the meaning of an oath, becoming synonymous with "truly." For this reason, it is answered with the "lam" (اللام), such as: "Truly (لا جرم) I shall surely do such-and-such." End quote. It has been mentioned that "jarm" (جرم) with two fatha marks means "cutting." Perhaps it was originally used for the conclusions of speech like the word "inevitably" (لا محالة), signifying that no cutter can cut this statement—that such-and-such is the case—just as a similar meaning is conceived for "inevitably." Thus, the meaning of the verse based on this is: Truly, in the Hereafter, they are the greatest losers.

The reason for their being the greatest losers in the Hereafter—if we assume they are greater losers relative to others among the people of disobedience—is that they ruined their souls by destroying and losing them through disbelief and obstinacy. Thus, there is no hope for their salvation from the Fire in the Hereafter, just as there was no hope for them to succeed in the world and be happy through faith as long as they remained obstinate. He, the Exalted, said: **الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** — *Those who have ruined their souls will not have faith* (6:12). And He, the Exalted, said regarding those whose hearing, sight, and hearts are sealed: **وَجَعَلْنا مِنْ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ سَدًّا وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ سَدًّا فَأَغْشَيْناهُمْ فَهُمْ لا يُبْصِرُونَ * وَسَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** — *And We have put a barrier before them and a barrier behind them, then We blindfolded them, so they do not see. It is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith* (36:9-10). He also said regarding the reason for the impossibility of their faith: **أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ وَأَضَلَّهُ اللهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ وَخَتَمَ عَلى سَمْعِهِ وَقَلْبِهِ وَجَعَلَ عَلى بَصَرِهِ غِشاوَةً فَمَنْ يَهْدِيهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ اللهِ** — *Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god and whom Allah has led astray knowingly, and set a seal upon his hearing and his heart, and put a blindfold on his sight? So who will guide him after Allah?* (45:23).

If we assume they are greater losers relative to the world, it is because through their disbelief and barring from the way of Allah, they were deprived of the happiness of life prepared for them by the true religion. Thus, they lost in the world just as they lost in the Hereafter. However, they are greater losers in the Hereafter because it is eternal and everlasting, whereas the world is but a little. He, the Exalted, said: **كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ ما يُوعَدُونَ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا ساعَةً مِنْ نَهارٍ** — *On the day they see what they are promised, it will be as if they had not stayed [in the world] but for an hour of a day* (46:35).

Furthermore, deeds intensify and double in the Hereafter through their results, as He, the Exalted, said: **وَمَنْ كانَ فِي هذِهِ أَعْمى فَهُوَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ أَعْمى وَأَضَلُّ سَبِيلاً** — *But whoever is blind in this [world] will be blind in the Hereafter [as well], and still more astray from the way* (17:72). The better of the two views is the first, because the apparent meaning of the verse is to restrict the "greatest losers" to them, rather than affirming their greater loss in the Hereafter compared to the world.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ وَأَخْبَتُوا إِلى رَبِّهِمْ** — *Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds and are humble before their Lord* (11:23) until the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "The khabt (الخبت) is a low-lying, tranquil piece of land. A man 'akhbata' (أخبت) when he headed for the khabt or descended into it, similar to 'ashala' and 'anjada'. Then 'ikhbat' (الإخبات) was used for the use of gentleness and humility. Allah, the Exalted, said: **وَأَخْبَتُوا إِلى رَبِّهِمْ**, and He said: **وَبَشِّرِ الْمُخْبِتِينَ** — *and give good news to the humble* — meaning the humble ones who do not wax proud against His worship. And His saying: **فَتُخْبِتَ لَهُ قُلُوبُهُمْ** — *and their hearts may be humble before Him* — meaning they become gentle and submissive." End quote.

Thus, what is intended by their being humble (إخباتهم) before Allah is their tranquility (اطمئنانهم) in Him, such that the faith in Him within their hearts does not waver; they do not deviate nor doubt, like the tranquil land that preserves what has settled within it. Therefore, there is no basis for what has been said—that the original was "akhbatu li-rabbihim" (humbled themselves *for* their Lord)—for that which carries the meaning of tranquility is made transitive with "to" (إلى) rather than "for" (اللام).

His, the Exalted, conditioning of faith and righteous deeds with being humble before Him indicates that what is intended by them is a specific group of believers. They are those among them who are tranquil in Allah, who possess insight from their Lord. This is what we pointed to at the beginning of the verses regarding His saying: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ** — *Is he who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord...* — that the verses compare two specific groups of people: the people of divine insight and those whose eye of insight is blind.

From here, the invalidity of what some commentators have mentioned becomes clear—that these seven verses, meaning from His saying: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ** until His saying: **أَفَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ**, are an explanation of the state of the two groups: those who disbelieve in the Quran and those who believe in it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مَثَلُ الْفَرِيقَيْنِ كَالْأَعْمى وَالْأَصَمِّ وَالْبَصِيرِ وَالسَّمِيعِ هَلْ يَسْتَوِيانِ مَثَلاً أَفَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ** — *The parable of the two parties is like the blind and the deaf, and the seeing and the hearing. Are they equal in comparison? Will you not then take admonition?* (11:24). A parable (المثل) is a description (الوصف), and it has become predominant in the "proverb" (المثل السائر), which is the explanation of a hidden meaning to the listener through a sensory matter or something like it, with which his mind is familiar and his understanding receives it, so as to transition through it to the intended intellectual meaning. What is intended by the "two parties" are those whose states were explained in the preceding verses. The rest is clear.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Ahmad ibn Umar al-Khallal, who said: I asked Abu al-Hasan (عليه السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَيَتْلُوهُ شاهِدٌ مِنْهُ** — *Is he who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord, and whom a witness from him follows?* He said: "The Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) is the witness from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the Messenger of Allah stands on a manifest proof from his Lord."

In the *Amali* of the Shaykh, with its chain to Abd al-Rahman ibn Kathir, from Ja'far ibn Muhammad, from his father, from his grandfather Ali ibn al-Husayn, from al-Hasan (عليه السلام), in a long sermon he delivered in the presence of Mu'awiyah—part of which was: "Matters proceeded and ages passed until Allah sent Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) for prophethood and chose him for the messengership, and revealed His Book to him, then commanded him to call to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic. My father was the first to respond to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and to His Messenger, and the first to believe and confirm Allah and His Messenger. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has said in His Book revealed to His sent Prophet: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَيَتْلُوهُ شاهِدٌ مِنْهُ**. Thus, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is the one who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord, and my father is the one who follows him and is a witness from him." The sermon.

**I say:** His (عليه السلام) speech is the best witness to what we have provided regarding the meaning of the verse—that his (عليه السلام) being intended as the witness is by way of application to the specific case (انطباق).

In *Basa'ir al-Darajat*, with its chain to al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah, who said: The Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) said: "If the cushion were folded for me and I sat upon it, I would judge between the people of the Torah by their Torah, and the people of the Gospel by their Gospel, and the people of the Criterion (الفرقان) — *the decisive standard that distinguishes truth from falsehood* — by their Criterion, with a judgment that ascends to Allah and shines. By Allah, no verse has been revealed in the Book of Allah by night or day except that I have known concerning whom it was revealed. And there is no one among those over whose head the razors have passed except that a verse has been revealed concerning him in the Book of Allah, leading him to Paradise or the Fire."

A man stood up to him and said: "O Commander of the Faithful, what is the verse that was revealed concerning you?" He said: "Have you not heard Allah saying: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَيَتْلُوهُ شاهِدٌ مِنْهُ**? The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) stands on a manifest proof from his Lord, and I am the witness for him and from him."

**I say:** al-Mufid narrated this meaning in *al-Amali* with a chain, and in *Kashf al-Ghummah* it is narrated without a chain from Abbad ibn Abd Allah al-Asadi from him (عليه السلام). al-Ayyashi in his *Tafsir* narrated it without a chain from Jabir from Abd Allah ibn Yahya from him (عليه السلام). Similarly, Ibn Shahrashub from al-Tabari with his chain to Jabir ibn Abd Allah from him (عليه السلام). Likewise from al-Asbagh, and from Zayn al-Abidin, al-Baqir, and al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) from him (عليه السلام).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, and Abu Nu'aym in *al-Ma'rifah* extracted from Ali ibn Abi Talib (رضي الله عنه) who said: "There is no man from Quraysh except that a portion of the Quran was revealed concerning him." A man said to him: "What was revealed concerning you?" He said: "Do you not read Surah Hud: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَيَتْلُوهُ شاهِدٌ مِنْهُ**? The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) stands on a manifest proof from his Lord, and I am a witness from him."

**I say:** In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from the *Tafsir* of al-Tha'labi with his chain to al-Sha'bi, tracing it back to Ali (عليه السلام) similarly. In it, from Ibn al-Maghazili, tracing it back to Abbad ibn Abd Allah from Ali (عليه السلام) similarly. Likewise from *Kunuz al-Rumuz* by al-Ras'ani similarly.

In it, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from another way from Ali (رضي الله عنه) who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "**أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ** is I, **وَيَتْلُوهُ شاهِدٌ مِنْهُ** is Ali."

**I say:** In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from Ibn al-Maghazili in the commentary on the verse from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) similarly.

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from Ibn al-Maghazili with his chain to Ali ibn Habis, who said: I and Abu Maryam entered upon Abd Allah ibn Ata. Abu Maryam said: "Relate to us the narration you related to me from Abu Ja'far." He said: "I was sitting with Abu Ja'far when the son of Abd Allah ibn Salam passed by us. I said: 'May I be your ransom, this is the son of the one who possesses the knowledge of the Book.' He said: 'No, but it is your companion Ali ibn Abi Talib, concerning whom verses from the Book of Allah, the Exalted, were revealed: **مَنْ عِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتابِ** (13:43), **أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَيَتْلُوهُ شاهِدٌ مِنْهُ** (11:17), and **إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** (5:55).'"

In it, also from Ibn Shahrashub from the memorizer (الحافظ) Abu Nu'aym through three chains from Ibn Abbas, who said: I heard Ali saying: "The saying of Allah, the Exalted: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَيَتْلُوهُ شاهِدٌ مِنْهُ** — the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) stands on a manifest proof, and I am the witness."

In it, also from Muwaffaq ibn Ahmad, who said: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَيَتْلُوهُ شاهِدٌ مِنْهُ**, Ibn Abbas said: "It is Ali; he testifies for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and he is from him."

**I say:** It was narrated from al-Tha'labi in his *Tafsir*, tracing it back to Ibn Abbas: "**أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَيَتْلُوهُ شاهِدٌ مِنْهُ** refers to Ali specifically."

**I say:** The author of *al-Manar* said in the commentary on the verse when mentioning the meanings of the witness: "Among them: that it is Ali (رضي الله عنه); the Shi'a narrate it and interpret it as the Imamate (الإمامة) — *the divine leadership of the Muslim community*. And it was narrated that he (كرم الله وجهه) was asked about it and denied it, interpreting it as his [the Prophet's] (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) tongue. Their opponents countered them with similar claims, saying it is Abu Bakr. Both are from interpretation by desire (التفسير بالهوى)." End quote. As for his saying: "the Shi'a narrate it," you have seen that its narrators from the Sunnis are more numerous than the Shi'a. As for his saying: "it is like its interpretation as Abu Bakr, from interpretation by desire," what has preceded regarding the meaning of the verse is sufficient for you in that regard; so refer back to it.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Zayd al-Shahham from Abu Abd Allah (عليه السلام), who said: I said to him: "We have a man among us called Kulayb; nothing comes from you except that he says: 'I submit (أنا أسلم).' So we named him Kulayb of Submission (كليب تسليم)." He (عليه السلام) prayed for mercy upon him and then said: "Do you know what submission (التسليم) is?" We remained silent. He said: "By Allah, it is being humble (الإخبات) — the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ وَأَخْبَتُوا إِلى رَبِّهِمْ**."

**I say:** al-Ayyashi narrated similarly in his *Tafsir*, as did al-Kashshi and the author of *al-Basa'ir* from Abu Usamah Zayd al-Shahham from him (عليه السلام).
